abstract: We report the synthesis and characterization of (NBu4)2Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, a three-dimensional network comprising Fe(III) centers bridged by paramagnetic semiquinoid linkers. This material exhibits a room-temperature conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm, ranking among the highest values reported to date for a metal–organic framework (MOF). Through a combination of spectroscopic techniques, slow-scan cyclic voltammetry, and temperature-dependent conductivity and magnetic susceptibility measurements, we demonstrate that this high electronic conductivity originates from ligand mixed-valency. Notably, UV-vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy provides the first evidence of Robin–Day Class II/III mixed valency within a MOF architecture. To explore the role of stoichiometry, we synthesized a reduced analog, Na0.9(NBu4)1.8Fe(III)2(dhbq)3. A comparative analysis reveals how the ratio of ligand redox states dictates the resulting electronic conductivity and magnetic ordering temperature. These findings establish transition metal–semiquinoid frameworks as a versatile platform for engineering tunable, long-range electronic communication in porous solids.
